meta.purpose: Define language patterns, common mistakes, and self-checks for speaking judgment.
meta.load_condition: Always load when preparing or reviewing a talk, presentation, or pitch.

preferred_terms.definition: The one thing the audience should remember after the talk.
The first 60 seconds that establish why the audience should care.
Content that directly serves the takeaway.

banned_terms.term: I'll try to keep this short
banned_terms.why: This phrase signals that the speaker knows the talk is too long but won't fix it. It requests patience instead of earning attention.
banned_terms.replace_with: Cut content until the talk is the right length, then start without apology.

banned_terms.why: If the audience can see it, you don't need to say it. If they can't, the visual failed.
banned_terms.replace_with: Point to the specific element and explain what it means, not that it exists.

banned_terms.why: Diminishers that undermine the speaker's authority before making a point.
banned_terms.replace_with: State the point directly without qualifiers.

banned_terms.term: sorry / I'm not an expert but
banned_terms.why: Pre-emptive apologies transfer the speaker's anxiety to the audience and undermine credibility.
banned_terms.replace_with: If you have something valuable to say, say it. If not, don't speak.

misunderstandings.wrong: A good presentation needs beautiful, detailed slides.
misunderstandings.correct: A good presentation needs a clear argument. Slides exist to amplify the argument. A talk with no slides and a clear message beats a talk with beautiful slides and no message.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Visual design is a multiplier: it makes a good talk better and a bad talk more visibly bad.
misunderstandings.why: New speakers invest time in slide aesthetics because it feels productive and avoids the harder work of structuring the argument.

misunderstandings.wrong: Nervousness means the speaker is unprepared or bad at speaking.
misunderstandings.correct: Nervousness means the speaker cares about the outcome. The goal is not to eliminate nerves — it is to channel the energy into genuine engagement with the audience.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Anxiety (fear of judgment) vs. excitement (anticipation of impact). The physiology is similar; the framing changes the outcome.
misunderstandings.why: Treating nervousness as a problem to solve creates more nervousness. Reframing it as energy to use makes it an asset.

misunderstandings.wrong: The more content covered, the more valuable the talk.
misunderstandings.correct: The more the audience retains and uses, the more valuable the talk. A single idea that changes behavior is worth more than twenty ideas that are forgotten.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Coverage vs. retention. The speaker's desire to 'cover everything' conflicts with the audience's ability to retain anything.
misunderstandings.why: Speakers fear leaving things out. Audiences fear wasting time. Meeting the audience's fear serves both parties.

self_check: Can I state the talk's one message in a single sentence the audience could repeat?
Does the opening give the audience a reason to care within the first 60 seconds?
If removing any slide, story, or section does not weaken the takeaway, should it be removed?
If the audience remembers nothing else, will they remember the one message?
